coredata.dc:title: Melamine modified carbon dots as high effective corrosion inhibitor for Q235 carbon steel in neutral 3.5 wt% NaCl solution
coredata.dc:creator: Zeng, Yuanxian
Kang, Lei
Wu, Ying
Wan, Shan
Liao, Bokai
Li, Nan
Guo, Xingpeng
coredata.dc:description: Melamine modified carbon dots (Me-CDs) was synthesized by hydrothermal method and used as effective corrosion inhibitor for Q235 carbon steel in 3.5 wt% sodium chloride (NaCl) solution. The microstructure and surface properties of Me-CDs were characterized using XRD, FTIR, XPS and TEM. The corrosion inhibition behavior and mechanism of Me-CDs for carbon steel were investigated using weight loss and electrochemical methods, in combination with some surface characterizations (including SEM, AFM and contact angle tester). Results showed that Me-CDs as mixed-type adsorbent inhibitor could effectively prevent carbon steel from corrosion, and the maximum corrosion inhibition efficiency can reach 92.36% at 200 mg/L. The absorption of Me-CDs conformed to Temkin adsorption model, involving both chemisorption and physisorption. Based on the results of characterization analysis, the chemisorption from multiple adsorption centers, physisorption by blocking and barrier effect, and the formation of hydrophobic film on the surface of carbon steel was proposed to clarify the corrosion inhibition mechanism of Me-CDs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Melamine modified carbon dots (Me-CDs) was synthesized by hydrothermal method and used as effective corrosion inhibitor for Q235 carbon steel in 3.5 wt% sodium chloride (NaCl) solution. The microstructure and surface properties of Me-CDs were characterized using XRD, FTIR, XPS and TEM. The corrosion inhibition behavior and mechanism of Me-CDs for carbon steel were investigated using weight loss and electrochemical methods, in combination with some surface characterizations (including SEM, AFM and contact angle tester). Results showed that Me-CDs as mixed-type adsorbent inhibitor could effectively prevent carbon steel from corrosion, and the maximum corrosion inhibition efficiency can reach 92.36% at 200 mg/L. The absorption of Me-CDs conformed to Temkin adsorption model, involving both chemisorption and physisorption. Based on the results of characterization analysis, the chemisorption from multiple adsorption centers, physisorption by blocking and barrier effect, and the formation of hydrophobic film on the surface of carbon steel was proposed to clarify the corrosion inhibition mechanism of Me-CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: The corrosion of metal material has been a worldwide problem due to its wide application in various fields [1–3]. Owing to the well mechanical performance, cost-effectiveness, abundant resources, and high recycling rate, Q235 carbon steel acts as one of the most important structural materials [4–6], such as buildings, bridge, drilling and production equipment, pipeline, etc. [7–10] During service period, Q235 carbon steel inevitably suffers from corrosion in chloride-containing occasions [11–13], which can damage the service performance of steel products and cause enormous economic loss. In this case, large numbers of corrosion inhibitors have been developed to slow down the corrosion rate of Q235 carbon steel [14-17 and thus prolong the equipment service life [14–16]. However, traditional high-performance corrosion inhibitors, such as metal salts (tungstate, chromate, etc.) and organic compounds, are harmful to human health and environment [17–19]. It’s difficult to meet current growing demand for the green production [20–21]. And developing some green and effective corrosion inhibitors is of great importance.

Carbon dots (CDs) are novel carbon-based nanomaterials, which have always increasingly received attention because of their low toxicity, desirable biocompatibility, simple synthesis, abundant raw materials, and prominent luminescence properties [22–24]. Some functionalized CDs have been applied as corrosion inhibitor for carbon steel in acidic environments [3,25–28]. For instance, Cui et al. [24] synthesized CDs corrosion inhibitor through a hydrothermal process using aminosalicylic acid as precursor, and firstly researched its anticorrosion property for carbon steel in 1 mol/L HCl solution. Results indicated that heteroatom (containing N, S, O, and P) doping or functional group (containing heteroatom) modifying could significantly improve the inhibition performance of CDs [3,5,29]. Ye et al. [6] synthesized functionalized CDs inhibitor using citric acid and imidazole, the synergistic inhibition effect between CDs and imidazole for Q235 steel in 1 mol/L HCl solution was discussed. Limited reports concerned functionalized CDs as corrosion inhibitor for carbon steel in neutral solutions. Cen et al. [4] prepared N, S co-doped CDs by a solvothermal method using thiourea and aminosalicylic acid, the anticorrosion behavior and the nano-sized effect of inhibitor on the corrosion inhibition efficiency for carbon steel in CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solution were discussed. So far, few investigations have reported the effect of nanostructure of functionalized CDs on the inhibition for carbon steel in neutral NaCl solutions.

Melamine and its derivatives possessed the analogical chemical structure of Schiff bases and can be applied as one kind of corrosion inhibitor [29–31]. However, melamine has a strong network structure due to the interaction of plentiful hydrogen bonding, which results in its poor solubility in common solvents [32]. The solubility and inhibition capability of melamine derivatives can be greatly improved by modifying or coordinating reactions, presenting powerful adsorption capability on the surface of the metal [30–35,33–35]. The introduction of functional groups containing heteroatom has been proven to be an effective strategy to further enhance the inhibition performance of CDs [4,6,29,36].

In this work, citric acid-based CDs was firstly modified using melamine via amidation reaction to prepare the functionalized CDs (Me-CDs). Me-CDs was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR). The inhibition property of Me-CDs for Q235 carbon steel in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution was evaluated using weight loss method, potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Surface characterizations of corrosion products were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) and water contact angle measurement. Besides, the relevant mechanism was illuminated by the Temkin adsorption isotherm for Me-CDs on the surface of carbon steel. Owing to the great electronegativity and electron-donating capacity of O and N atoms, Me-CDs nanoparticles could adsorb on the carbon steel surface through the coordination bonds formed between Me-CDs nanoparticles and iron atom. The formation of hydrophobic film on the surface of carbon steel was proposed to make clear the corrosion inhibition mechanism.

Citric acid monohydrate, 4-dimethylaminopyridine, N, N'-dicyclohexyl-carbodiimide, triethylamine and melamine were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Acetone, alcohol and sodium chloride were sourced from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. 3.5 wt% NaCl solution was prepared using deionized (DI) water and analytical-grade sodium chloride. Q235 carbon steel specimens used in this work mainly contains the following elements (wt. %): C 0.34, Si 0.29, S 0.013, P 0.015, Mn 1.39, Cr 0.45, Mo 0.3, Ni 0.028, and Fe (the remaining proportion). The specimens were processed into rectangular with dimensions of 10 mm × 10 mm × 3 mm for the weight loss test. The specimens were cut into a cylinder shape with a 1.0 cm2 exposed face to solution for electrochemical measurements, some other surfaces were sealed by ethoxy resin. Prior to tests, the exposed surface was burnished with 180, 400, 800 and 1200 grit abrasive papers, respectively, cleaned with deionized water, then degreased with alcohol and acetone, and finally dried by cold-blast air. The well-polished specimens were then stored in a vacuum desiccator before tests.

CDs were synthesized based on hydrothermal method, and the schematic diagram for preparation procedure is presented in Fig. 1 . In a nutshell, citric acid monohydrate (2.1 g) was dissolved in 20 mL of ethanol and 1 mL of triethylamine with stirring, then transferred into Teflon cup in the steel reaction autoclave and heated at 190 °C for 12 h. Some brown solutions were obtained after being naturally cooled to the room temperature and stored in sealed sample bottle.

Ethanol (20 mL) and N, N'-dicyclohexyl-carbodiimide (1 g) were successively added into previously procured solutions and stirred for 5 min, then 1.5 g of 4-dimethylaminopyridine and 1.26 g of melamine were sequentially added into the above solution and simultaneously stirred for 5 min. Subsequently, the mixed liquor was heated to 85 °C, stirred for 6 h and then cooled to room temperature naturally. The reaction mixtures were filtered using a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and used to get rid of insoluble particles. The colature was purified using DI water with dialysis tubes (formula weight cut-off ∼ 500 Da) for 24 h, and DI water was changed at intervals of 8 h. Finally, colature was concentrated by evaporation and ulteriorly freeze-dried under vacuum, brownish-black solids were gained.

To measure the inhibition behavior of Me-CDs for Q235 carbon steel in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, the fresh 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions containing various concentrations (50, 100, 150, 200 mg/L) of Me-CDs were prepared and stored in volumetric flask. The initial conductivity of each solution was tested with a JENCO Model 3173 conductivity tester. And the corresponding pH value was monitored with a PHS-3C pH meter (Rex Instrument Factory, Shanghai, China). The conductivity value is 63.52, 63.70, 64.10, 64.35, 64.82 mS/cm and the pH value is 6.01, 7.65, 8.69, 9.48, 10.16 severally, when the concentration of Me-CDs reaches 0, 50, 100, 150, 200 mg/L in the 3.5 wt% NaCl solution.

The chemical structure of Me-CDs was measured using TENSOR II- Hyperion2000 FTIR. The chemical component and chemical bonding of Me-CDs on the surface of carbon steel were analyzed using XPS (AXIS-ULTRA DLD-600 W) with Al Kα excitation source. XRD patterns was characterized using X’Pert PRO (PW3040/60) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). TEM images were acquired from JEOLJEM-2100F. SEM (JEOL JSM-7001F) and AFM (Bruker Dimension icon) were adopted to observe the surface morphology of Q235 carbon steel after corrosion test.

The corrosion inhibition behavior of Me-CDs for Q235 carbon steel was measured by electrochemical workstation (CS-310H, Wuhan Corrtest Instruments Corp., Ltd.) with a traditional three-electrode system. The working electrode was Q235 carbon steel with an exposed area of 1.0 cm2. The saturated calomel electrode (SCE) and a platinum plate with an area of 2.5 cm2 were adopted as reference electrode and auxiliary electrode, respectively. All measurements were implemented at 25 ± 0.5 °C in a thermostatic water bath. Before each electrochemical test, open circuit potential (OCP) tests were conducted for 1 h to achieve a steady-state.

The EIS test was conducted under the open-circuit state with an amplitude of 10 mV and a frequency range of 104 Hz ∼ 10-2 Hz. The obtained data were analyzed by ZView software. To ensure the reproducibility, each test was conducted for three times. As for PDP measurements, the scanning range of −250 mV ∼ 250 mV relative to OCP with a scanning rate of 0.5 mV/s was carried out, and Tafel slope data were fitted and analyzed by CView software.

Prior to test, carbon steel specimens were polished to 1200 grit using abrasive papers and weighted thrice at various time intervals through analytical balance with a precision of 0.1 mg. And then, the specimens were immersed in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution with and without various concentrations of inhibitor. After 24 h immersion, the specimens were placed into the rust remover to wipe off corrosion products, then washed by deionized water and ethyl alcohol in sequence. Ultimately, the specimens were dried by cold-blast air and re-weighed three times accurately to calculate the average corrosion rates. The corrosion rate (ν corr ) can be calculated by the following equation:
(1)
ν
corr
=
Δ W
S × t

Where ΔW presents the average weight loss (g) of the three specimens before and after test. S values are the total immersion area (m2) in the corrosive medium, and t values are the immersion time (h).

Corrosion inhibition efficiency (η) for Me-CDs could be defined by the following equation:
(2)

Before SEM and AFM tests, the specimens were polished to 1200 grit using abrasive papers and soaked in the 3.5 wt% NaCl solution with 200 mg/L of Me-CDs at 25 °C for 24 h, then were washed after 24 h immersion and dried in a thermostatic vacuum drier. The corrosion morphology of carbon steel specimen surface and the morphology and dimension of Me-CDs inhibitor film on carbon steel surface were observed through Dimension JSM-7001F SEM. Bruker Dimension Icon AFM instrument was conducted to acquire the 2Dand 3D morphology of carbon steel surface with and without Me-CDs inhibitor.

The water contact angle on Q235 carbon steel surface was measured using liquid droplet method by contact angle tester (JC2000, Powereach). The specimens were soaked in the 3.5 wt% NaCl solution with 0, 50, 100, 150, and 200 mg/L of Me-CDs at 25 °C for 24 h before contact angle tests. After the carbon steel specimens were washed, and dried by cold-blast air, distilled water droplet (2 μL) was dripped to the specimen surface to acquire the contact angle. The eventual contact angle was computed by the average value of paralleling measurements in diverse three regions of the specimen.

In situ AFM tests were implemented at room temperature with a commercial AFM system (SPA400, Seiko Instruments Inc.,100 μm top-view scanning head). The force curves were detected in contact mode using Si3N4 tips with spring constant of 0.02 Nm−1 and the imaging process were enforced in tapping mode using Si tips with spring constant of 3 Nm−1.The samples were immersed in solutions for 2 h, then were slightly rinsed with distilled water and dried in the air for measurement.

The chemical functional groups of melamine and Me-CDs were characterized by FTIR spectrum. The spectra of melamine were used as comparing absorption peaks with Me-CDs to identify the variation in the chemical structures of reactants. Fig. 2 (a) shows the FTIR spectra of melamine and Me-CDs. In the case of Me-CDs, the absorption peaks at 814 and 1026 cm−1 were typical absorption for triazine ring attributed to out-of-plane ring bending and ring breathing [37–39], separately, and a series of characteristic absorption peaks at 585, 1184, 1196, 1400, 1436, 1551, 1641, 1653, 1705 and 3420 cm−1 were ascribed to CNH, COH, CN (exocyclic), COO–, CN (triazine ring), NCN (triazine ring), NCO, –NH2, CO and NH, respectively [40]. The CNH, triazine ring, CN, NCN, –NH2 and NH bonds were derived from melamine, while the COH, COO– and CO bonds were from CDs. Among them, the formation of NCO bond indicated that the carboxyl of CDs successfully reacted with the amidogen of melamine.

The XRD diffraction patterns of Me-CDs sample (Fig. 2(b)) appeared some of characteristic diffraction peaks indexed to the (110), (011), (210), (002), (−301), (−311), and (310) crystal planes, which are consistent with most characteristic peaks of melamine (JCPDS card No. 24–1654). It was indicated that melamine reacted with the CDs, which was well in agreement with the result of FTIR spectra. Furthermore, the XRD pattern of Me-CDs presented a broad and weak diffraction peak at 2θ around 26.6°, which might be vest in the characteristic peak (002 facet) of graphite carbon [41–42]. This can be attributed to the interlayer spacing of approximately 0.3348 nm calculated by Bragg's Law, which revealed a quite highly ordered crystal texture and agreed with the result of TEM. As the particle diameter increased, the interlayer spacing of the layered graphene decreases due to the nano-sized effect [43–44]. Therefore, the presence of triazine rings and nano-size effect in Me-CDs nanoparticle resulted in a smaller interlayer spacing compared with that in general graphite layer (0.34 nm) [4,45]. A weak diffraction peak appeared at approximately 23.5°, probably resulting from residues or contaminants in the Me-CDs [46].

The high-resolution TEM was carried out to analyze the morphology and structure of Me-CDs. As revealed in Fig. 4a, Me-CDs were uniform spherical nanoparticles with diameters of 10 ∼ 30 nm and well-dispersed without clustering. The modification using triazine ring led to the particle diameter of Me-CDs (Fig. 2(c)) was greater than that of non-modified CDs (∼1–10 nm) [3,5,45]. High-resolution TEM image (Fig. 2(d)) displayed the high crystallinity of the Me-CDs. The lattice spacing of Me-CDs was 0.25 and 0.33 nm, which showed in conformity to the reported values of the interplanar distance of graphene (102 facet) and the interlayer spacing between the graphene layers (002 facet) [4,47–48], separately.

ce:section.ce:label: 3.2 wt
ce:section.ce:para:
The average weight loss (ΔW), corrosion rates (
ν
corr
), and inhibition efficiencies (η) of Q235 carbon steel in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution at 25 °C with the addition of various concentrations of are summarized in Table 1 . Compared with corrosion rate in solution without Me-CDs, the corrosion rates of Q235 carbon steel decreased significantly in the presence of Me-CDs. And the inhibition efficiency increased gradually with the increase of corrosion inhibitor concentrations.

The corrosion morphologies of Q235 carbon steel after 24 h immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution in the presence and absence of Me-CDs were depicted in Fig. 4 . As shown in Fig. 4a, there existed obvious scratches caused by polishing. In Fig. 4b, formation of some deep corrosion pits indicated severe corrosion of carbon steel caused by aggressive media. After the addition of 200 mg/L Me-CDs, few corrosion pits can be observed on the carbon steel surface (Fig. 4c). The serried inhibitor films (Me-CDs nanoparticles) were observed by high-resolution SEM, as shown in Fig. 4d. Me-CDs nanoparticles with the dimension of 10 ∼ 30 nm covered on the carbon steel surface and formed a protective film through effective adsorption due to the strong interaction between Me-CDs nanoparticles and the Fe atom. It was mainly attributed to that Me-CDs had several active sites, including OH, NH2, CO, NCO, COO- and triazine ring (Fig. 2a), contributing to the stronger adsorption of Me-CDs nanoparticles on the carbon steel surface and thus alleviating corrosion.

AFM can be a powerful instrument used for obtaining the surface 3D morphology, roughness (Ra), height profile distribution, and the physicochemical property. Fig. 5 showed the AFM results of the carbon steel specimens after 24 h immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions without and with 200 mg/L Me-CDs inhibitor at 25 °C. As shown in Fig. 5, the 3D and 2D AFM patterns in Fig. 5(d and e) were smoother in comparison with that in Fig. 5(a and b). Owing to the severe erosion of samples, the surface of carbon steel was greatly uneven after 24 h immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution and the Ra value reached up to 174.1 nm (Fig. 5(a)). By comparison, the Ra value stupendously decreased to 9.9 nm when 200 mg/L Me-CDs inhibitor (Fig. 5(d)) was added, indicating that the Me-CDs inhibitor could retard corrosion of carbon steel. The 3D pattern (Fig. 5d) displayed that the Me-CDs nanoparticles were serried accumulation, forming the dense protective film on the carbon steel surface. And the stack height of Me-CDs nanoparticles was up to 44.8 nm. In accordance with the height distribution image, the relative altitude of most zones ranged from about 15 nm to 35 nm, meaning that the multilayer cumulation overlaid. In height profile figures of the defined area from Fig. 5e and f, the stack height of Me-CDs ranged from about 15 nm to 25 nm, with Me-CDs particle size of around 10 nm to 30 nm in HR-TEM. All these were further confirmed that the inhibited film formed by Me-CDs nanoparticles densely overlay the surface of Q235 carbon steel.

AFM can be also used to quantify intuitively the adhesive force between adsorption film and solid surface by measuring force-distance curve [54–55]. The measured representative force-distance curve of AFM is presented in Fig. 5(g). At large probe tip-sample distances, the force experienced by the tip is null. When the tip approaches the sample surfaces, it will experience attractive forces (negative) between the area from point a to b. When the attractive forces surpass the spring forces of the cantilever, the tip will jump into touching with the surface of sample at point b. After the repulsive forces (plus) gradually increase, the increasing rate is related to the physical characters of the surface and the interaction forces on the interface. While retracting the tip from the sample, the curve displays a hysteresis referred to as the adhesion “pull-off” force [55–56]. As the spring forces of the cantilever exceed the adhesive attraction forces, the tip jumps out of contacting from the surface of sample and a maximal adhesive force is detected at point c. In general, no adhesive force feature is measured on a pure rigid surface in the force-distance curve. However, the adhesive force can be detected owing to the adhesive function between the sample and the tip of the probe when the sample surface is wrapped by organic adsorption film [56]. The force curves were measured on the Q235 carbon steel surfaces after 2 h immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution with and without 200 mg/L Me-CDs, are given in Fig. 5(h). To minimize errors brought by uncertain factors, the measurements were repeated and 7 force-distance curves were detected in every selective site. The representative force-curves were picked out and the average of adhesive forces were computed. As shown in Fig. 5(h), the adhesive forces of the Q235 carbon steel surfaces immersed in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution containing 200 mg/L Me-CDs were much larger than that in the blank solution. The average values for adhesive forces were 69.55 nN and 31.12 nN, respectively. The results proved that adsorbed Me-CDs organic film constructed protective films on the carbon steel surface.

The water contact angle on the metal surface can be perceived as a parameter to analyze the hydrophobic performance and anticorrosion ability of inhibitors [4,57]. Fig. 6 shows the water contact angle on the Q235 carbon steel specimens prior and after immersion tests in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution containing various concentrations of inhibitor at 25 °C for 24 h. The contact angle is 60.9 ± 1.6° and 56.7 ± 1.2° before and after immersion separately. The hydrophilicity of carbon steel surface was enhanced after immersing in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution without Me-CDs. And the contact angle is 64.5 ± 0.9°, 67.4 ± 1.1°, 73.3 ± 1.2°, and 97.4 ± 1.6° with the addition of 50, 100, 150, and 200 mg/L Me-CDs, respectively. Results indicated that the water contact angle increased with the increasing concentration of Me-CDs, suggesting that the hydrophobic adsorbed film formed on the carbon steel surface by Me-CDs nanoparticles. This can be attributed to the hydrophobicity of nano-graphite carbon ring and triazine ring in the Me-CDs. This hydrophobic film could seclude carbon steel surface from corrosive media and thus inhibit the corrosion.

Fig. 7 (a) displays the OCP curves of Q235 carbon steel in 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions with different concentrations of Me-CDs. All OCP curves reached the stable state with the immersion time increasing. Compared with organic corrosion inhibitors, the stable-state of Me-CDs can be achieved after a longer time, which may be due to the slower transfer rate of Me-CDs owing to the larger particle size and heavier weight [4]. The OCP values of Q235 steel in 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions containing Me-CDs moved to the positive position than that in 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions, and the OCP values increased as the concentration of Me-CDs increased. Nevertheless, when the concentration of Me-CDs was higher than 150 mg/L, the OCP value increased slightly, which might be caused by the maximum adsorption of Me-CDs on the carbon steel surface. Moreover, as the apparent positive shifts for OCP value with the addition with Me-CDs. It is well-founded to deem that Me-CDs proved main corrosion inhibitory effect on the anodic half reactions [46]. Generally, the CDs-type corrosion inhibitors can act as an anodic corrosion inhibitor, which effectively block the anodic active sites and suppress the anodic reactions [3–4,24].

Fig. 8 represents the Nyquist plots and Bode plots for Q235 carbon steel measured in the absence and presence of Me-CDs with different concentrations for different immersion time. The EIS was implemented continually as a function of time to real time observation of corrosion inhibition behavior of Me-CDs. The diameter of capacitive reactance arc notably increased with the addition of Me-CDs, implying that Me-CDs formed a valid adsorption film on the carbon steel surface and the corrosion of carbon steel in these systems was restrained. This can be due to the surface of carbon steel was covered by more inhibitor adsorption film as the concentration of Me-CDs increased [5]. All Nyquist plots of EIS were composed of two capacitive loops, corresponding to the charge transfer process and relaxation process. Due to the time constant of the two capacitive arcs were similar, the capacitive arcs were superimposed so that the impedance plot exhibited a semicircular-like arc. Once adding the Me-CDs, the impedance plots exhibited flat capacitive loops which were mostly correlated with the adsorption of Me-CDs and the formation of a dense adsorption film made up of Me-CDs nanoparticles. Fe ion merely interpenetrated the adsorbed film in brush-fire region and formed the anode current. The parameters of dispersion impact were reduced with the uneven distribution of current to flatten the capacitive loops [4,50]. EIS plots in the blank systems (Fig. 8a), the capacitive reactance arc was ascertained by double-layer capacitance in the low-frequency district, and the other capacitive reactance arc at middle-high frequency was attributed to membrane resistance of corrosion products. In addition, the diameter of capacitive loops gradually shrinks with the process of test time, this is mainly linked with the prior dissolution of ferrite [50]. Then, the corrosion inhibiting effect of Me-CDs can be observed in Fig. 8(c, e, g, and i). For instance, the diameter of capacitive loops with Me-CDs inhibiter systems were much larger than that under blank condition. The diameter was visibly expanding as the concentration of Me-CDs increased, manifesting heightened corrosion inhibition efficiency for defending Q235 carbon steel. This implied that the charge transfer process was controlled in the Me-CDs inhibitor systems principally because of the adsorption of Me-CDs inhibitor on the Q235 carbon steel surface which clogged charge transfer pathway. It was worth mentioning that the diameter of capacitive loops diminished initially and enlarged finally with the enhancement of test time in the presence of different concentrations (Table 2 and Fig. 8(c, e, g, and i)), indicating that the Me-CDs inhibiter could firmly adsorb onto Q235 carbon steel surface to protect carbon steel from being eroded.

In the Bode phase angle curves (Fig. 8(b, d, f, h, j)), two time constants appeared in Bode-phase angle plots, which were in accordance with the film capacitance at middle-high frequency and double-layer capacitance at low frequency, respectively. Obviously, Bode-phase angle curves showed the broader signal with the increase of concentration of Me-CDs, just like two time constants overlap, pointing the dense inhibitor film with fewer porous. In addition, the maximum phase angle increased as the concentration of Me-CDs increased, indicating that Me-CDs just obstructed some active sites on the carbon steel surface. And the dense film with diminutive void dimension blocked the transfer of corrosive substances [4]. For Bode plots, the impedance modulus (|Z|) in the low-frequency area (0.01 Hz) was inversely proportional to corrosion rate of metal [29], increase in |Z| deduces more remarkable corrosion resistance. Markedly, the |Z| 0.01 Hz showed an upward trend with the increase of Me-CDs concentration. The |Z| 0.01 Hz value of Q235 carbon steel for 24 h immersion in the absence of Me-CDs was 1011.5 Ω·cm2 while it was 6962.3 Ω·cm2 with the addition of 200 mg/L Me-CDs, meaning formation of protective film of Me-CDs on the carbon steel surface. These results showed that the Me-CDs could display the outstanding corrosion inhibition for Q235 carbon steel, which could block the immediate contact between the corrosion species and carbon steel. To fit the parameters in EIS curves and analyze the EIS data and further research the electrochemical corrosion mechanism, the EIS curves were fitted using the electrical equivalent circuit model (Fig. 9 ). In the equivalent circuit, the R s, R f, and R ct, represented the solution resistance, the film resistance, and the charge transfer resistance, respectively. CPE f and CPE ct were the constant phase element, representing the film capacitance and the double-layer capacitance, severally. The fitting parameters of EIS are summarized in Table 2, In the absence of Me-CDs, the R f and R ct values decreased gradually with the test time increasing, reflecting the weak shielded scale and stepped-up corrosion rate, whereas the CPE ct and CPE f gradually enhanced with the test time, which was ascribed to the added roughness of scale and carbon steel surface. However, the CPE ct and CPE f values of carbon steel in Me-CDs solution were visibly reduced than that in the absence of Me-CDs at the same immersion time, which corresponded to the adsorption of Me-CDs on the carbon steel surface. These were due to the fact that the corrosion species on the surface of carbon steel were gradually displaced by Me-CDs. Because the permittivity of Me-CDs was much lower than that of water and the thickness of the absorbed Me-CDs film was much larger. The bare area of carbon steel decreased after the addition of Me-CDs, thereby the interface capacitance containing Me-CDs decreased obviously [5,29]. The R ct value enlarged markedly after the addition of Me-CDs inhibiters, and enhanced with the concentration of Me-CDs increased, pointing that the Me-CDs could form an effective protective film on the surface of Q235 carbon steel to prevent carbon steel caused by corrosion.

The PDP curves for Q235 carbon steel in 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions without and with various concentrations of Me-CDs after 24 h immersion were presented in Fig. 10 . The average values of polarization curve parameters were fitted via weak polarization area, including E corr, i corr, anodic Tafel slope (βa ), cathodic Tafel slope (βc ), corrosion inhibition efficiency ( η ), and degree of surface coverage ( θ ), were shown in Table 3 . Combined with Fig. 10 and Table 3, when the Me-CDs were added, the corrosion potentials (E corr) moved toward the positive direction and the corrosion current density (i corr) distinctly decreased compared with that under the blank condition, indicating that the Me-CDs was the anodic inhibitor and can effectively restrain the anodic reactions. This could be associated to the adsorbed inhibitor film on the Q235 carbon steel surface, which may block the active sites and thus suppress corrosion of carbon steel [4,25].

As shown in Fig. 10, with the addition of Me-CDs,

Me-CDs inhibitor had no apparent desorption phenomenon, which could be attributed to strong absorption between Me-CDs nanoparticles and carbon steel surface. This consequence was in accordance with the analysis of SEM and EIS data, indicating that the Me-CDs nanoparticles showed an outstanding inhibiting effect on Q235 carbon steel due to its multiple adsorption center. Clearly, values for E corr and OCP showed the same variation trend with the rising concentration of Me-CDs. The

Furthermore, the θ and η increased with the Me-CDs concentration increased. For example, when the Me-CDs concentration was 200 mg/L, η reached up to 93.18%, which was close to the results of weight loss and EIS tests. Compared with inhibition efficiency calculated from EIS (IE EIS ) and PDP (
η
Tafel
) measurements from some previous studies, the inhibition efficiency of Me-CDs was relatively higher under similar conditions (Table 4 ).

The adsorption isotherm can be applied to analyze the adsorption behavior of inhibitor and clarify the inhibition mechanism. The experimental data acquired from PDP were adopted to fit adsorption isotherm, and it was found to fit well with ‘‘Temkin” adsorption isotherm. The Temkin adsorption isotherm has the following form [66–67]:
(6)

where K ads is the adsorption–desorption equilibrium constant of inhibitor, C inh is the inhibitor concentration in the bulk solution, θ is the surface coverage and a is the lateral interaction coefficient. a can be used to describe the interaction and the nonuniformity for inhibitor molecules in the adsorption layer on metal surface, taking into consideration the repulsion (a < 0) or attraction (a > 0) between the adsorbed particles. For a = 0 (no interaction), the adsorption isotherm is equal to the Langmuir isotherm.

The linear correlation coefficient (R2) was 0.99176, indicating that the adsorption process of Me-CDs nanoparticles on carbon steel surface in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution obeyed the Temkin adsorption isotherm model. Meanwhile, negative values (-1.2329) of “a” manifested the greater lateral repulsive force between adsorbed Me-CDs inhibitors as well as those among Me-CDs nanoparticles, such as chloride ions and water molecules existing in the adsorption layer. Me-CDs inhibitors could replace chloride ions and water molecules to form a dense barrier film on the surface of carbon steel. This can be attributed to the strong adsorption of Me-CDs and the hydrophobic nanostructure in the Me-CDs, as showed in contact angle test results (Fig. 6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Corrosion inhibition mechanism by Me-CDs nanoparticle
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 12 exhibited a schematic diagram for the corrosion inhibition mechanism of Me-CDs inhibitor for Q235 carbon steel in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The corrosion process for carbon steel in chloride-containing solutions can be described as following: due to the smaller radius of Cl- and strong penetrating ability, it can preferentially adsorb on the metal surface, and Fe atoms were then activated by Cl-. Due to the dissolved oxygen has a strong ability to gain electrons and the activated Fe atoms easily were oxidized to form Fe2+ by the loss of electrons. Afterwards, the hydrolysis of Fe2+ produces H+ to accelerate the dissolution of Fe.

In general, adsorption and coordination interaction between organic inhibiters and carbon steel surface were deemed to be considerable anticorrosion mechanisms. The chemical adsorption was attributed to the heteroatoms such as N and O containing the lone pair electrons, which could readily fill in the unoccupied 3d-orbital outside the Fe atoms, and formed the coordinate-covalent bonds between heteroatoms and surface of carbon steel. As displayed in FTIR and XPS spectrums (Fig. 2(a) and Fig. 3), the as-prepared Me-CDs has numbers of active groups, including amino, oxhydryl, carboxyl, triazine ring, and amido bond, which can act as the multiple adsorption centers. The existence of Fe-N bond in XPS spectrum elucidated that some of the active groups are bonding on the surface of carbon steel by coordination bonds, which can be firmly adsorbed on carbon steel and thus retarding the invasion of corrosive media. Based on Temkin adsorption isotherms, the adsorption behavior for Me-CDs on the surface of carbon steel was belong to physicochemical adsorption (−40 kJ/mol <
Δ G
ads
0
< − 20 kJ/mol). The physical adsorption was mainly ascribed to the effect of agglomeration and multilayer accumulation of graphite carbon ring in the Me-CDs on the surface of steel (Fig. 12), also as showed in HR-SEM pictures and AFM result (Fig. 4(g) and Fig. 5(h)). Hence, the remaining Me-CDs nanoparticles in the solution diffused to the bareness point of carbon steel surface and chemisorbed layer, then the corrosive species were driven out of the carbon steel /solution interface and displaced by Me-CDs nanoparticles due to the existence of the weaker lateral repulsive force between them. The polarization curve (Fig. 10) indicated that Me-CDs serve as an anodic-type adsorbent inhibitor, effectively restraining the dissolution of anode for carbon steel. The results of force curves and the contact angle tests (Figs. 5 and 6) also illustrated the formation of dense hydrophobic film on the surface of carbon steel by adsorbed Me-CDs nanoparticles. In a word, the high inhibition efficiency of Me-CDs is correlated with chemisorption from multiple adsorption center, physisorption by barrier effect and hydrophobic nanostructure in the Me-CDs.

In summary, the Me-CDs was successfully prepared using hydrothermal method and firstly applied as a new corrosion inhibitor for Q235 carbon steel in neutral NaCl solution. Electrochemical methods and surface characterizations have been carried out to study the microstructure, inhibition effect and mechanism of Me-CDs in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The main conclusions can be drawn as follows:
1.
Me-CDs was an anodic-type inhibitor for Q235 carbon steel in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution and the inhibition efficiency can reach a maximum of 92.36% at 200 mg/L.
2.
The corrosive species can be displaced and driven out of the carbon steel/solution interface by physicochemical adsorption of Me-CDs nanoparticles, which conformed to Temkin adsorption model.
3.
The hydrophobic Me-CDs film can firmly adhere on the steel carbon surface through the coordinate-covalent bond and multilayer accumulation effect. It functioned as blocking the active sites and geometric blocking, avoiding the direct contact between corrosion media and carbon steel surface.

Shan Wan: Visualization, Conceptualization. Bokai Liao: Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Nan Li: Visualization, Conceptualization, Supervision.
